retrieved_docs.title: Methacholine Challenge Test -- Anatomy and Physiology
retrieved_docs.content: Methacholine is a nonselective muscarinic receptor agonist that acts directly on airway smooth muscle receptors to induce bronchoconstriction. [7] Methacholine also indirectly stimulates mucus cell secretion. Methacholine is more useful for testing than acetylcholine due to its longer duration of action. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Muscarinic Receptor -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: There are an estimated 358 million patients in the world affected by asthma. Asthma symptoms of dyspnea are secondary to bronchoconstriction and smooth muscle constriction. The increased parasympathetic activity can account for this constriction, including overstimulation of muscarinic receptors by increased release of acetylcholine. [16] M3 receptors mainly control the contraction of airway smooth muscle. M2 receptors function by inhibiting the action of beta-adrenergic stimulated relaxation. [17] In asthma, there is an increased level of eosinophils in the airway. Eosinophils release major basic protein, which in turn binds to M2 receptors. Because the M2 receptors are bound, acetylcholine cannot bind to the receptors, and relaxation of the airway becomes difficult; this allows the unbound acetylcholine to bind to M3 receptors, stimulating constriction of the airways thus producing asthma symptoms. [16]

retrieved_docs.title: Pathophysiology Of Asthma -- Related Testing
retrieved_docs.content: To diagnose asthma, clinicians can do a variety of tests. Tests must determine: expiratory airflow limitation, documentation of reversible obstruction, and rule out any exclusion of an alternative diagnosis. Assessing the patient’s obstruction via spirometry and checking for a reversible change after a bronchodilator is suggestive of the diagnosis of asthma. By using spirometry, physicians can diagnose asthma as well as the severity of obstruction. [20] The definition of the obstruction of air is spirometry of FEV1 less than 0.8, and the FEV1/FVC ratio is less than 0.70. The worse the asthma is, the lower the number is for FEV1 and the FEV1/FVC ratio. Another method of testing, if spirometry is normal, is bronchoprovocation testing with methacholine. Methacholine is a muscarinic agonist, which in the lungs constricts smooth muscle via M1, M2, and M3 receptors. [21] Methacholine can determine the severity of bronchoconstriction. Allergy testing is not necessarily useful in diagnosing asthma, but it can help identify certain triggers that worsen it.

retrieved_docs.title: Methacholine Challenge Test -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Methacholine challenge testing is a bronchoprovocation technique used to evaluate airway hyperresponsiveness, particularly in patients with suspected asthma who exhibit atypical symptoms or have normal baseline pulmonary function tests and spirometry results. Methacholine, a derivative of acetylcholine, directly induces bronchoconstriction by stimulating airway smooth muscle receptors. In some cases, hyperresponsiveness may be the only objective sign of asthma, making this test a valuable diagnostic tool. The procedure involves stepwise administration of methacholine, followed by spirometry to measure the forced expiratory volume in 1 second. This process continues until either a 20% reduction in the forced expiratory volume in 1 second or a 35% to 40% reduction in specific airways conductance is observed. The dose required to cause a 20% reduction is known as the provocation dose (PD20). A PD20 of 200 µg, or a provocation concentration (PC20) of 8 mg/mL or less, indicates a positive result (100 µg or 4 mg/mL for specific airway conductance).

retrieved_docs.title: Methacholine Challenge Test -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Methacholine challenge testing is a nonspecific bronchoprovocation method used to assess airway hyperresponsiveness, aiding in the diagnosis of asthma, particularly when baseline spirometry is normal but clinical suspicion remains. Methacholine, a cholinergic agonist, induces bronchoconstriction by stimulating airway smooth muscle receptors. The test involves administering increasing doses of methacholine with serial spirometry to measure FEV 1 . A drop in FEV 1 of 20% or more indicates a positive result. This highly sensitive test has a strong negative predictive value, making it especially useful for ruling out asthma. However, due to the potential for false positives in conditions such as chronic obstructive pulmonary disease and allergic rhinitis, results should be interpreted in the context of the overall clinical picture. Emergency resuscitation equipment and trained personnel must be present during testing to manage potential bronchospasm.

retrieved_docs.title: Asthma Medications -- Clinical Significance -- Short and Long-Acting Inhaled Muscarinic Antagonists
retrieved_docs.content: Anticholinergic medicines, like beta-2 agonists, have 2 classes of either short-acting or long-acting duration of effect. Shorter-acting medicines typically used include ipratropium bromide, and longer-acting anticholinergic medicines include tiotropium, aclidinium, glycopyrronium, and umeclidinium. These medications function by binding to and blocking neural signals from parasympathetic muscarinic receptors M1, M2, and M3. M1 receptors are found on the cholinergic ganglia and function to modulate the neural transmission of a parasympathetic signal. M2 receptors are found on postganglionic nerve bulbs of parasympathetic nerve fibers. Blockage of this receptor functions to decrease acetylcholine release from the postganglionic nerve endings with a net decrease in signal transmission. M3 receptors are found on smooth muscle cells, mucosal glands, and vascular endothelium along the airways. Blockage of these receptors leads to a decrease in bronchoconstriction, mucus secretion from glandular tissues, and edema of the mucosal linings. While there is no specificity for which muscarinic receptor subtype is inhibited, the primary effect on asthma is found from inhibiting M3 receptors. Blocking of the receptor leads to a decrease in cyclic guanosine monophosphate (cGMP) content within the smooth muscle cells. In turn, there is a decrease in calcium content that activates protein kinase A inactivating myosin light-chain kinase and activating myosin light-chain phosphatase leading to smooth muscle relaxation. Decreases in intracellular calcium also function to decrease mucous secretion from glandular cells. [8] [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Muscarinic Agonists -- Indications -- Direct Agonists
retrieved_docs.content: Methacholine is most commonly used to diagnose asthma or bronchial hyperactivity. Methacholine stimulates the muscarinic receptor in the airway when inhaled and induces bronchoconstriction, and increases tracheobronchial secretions. The lower the dose it takes for methacholine to induce bronchoconstriction, the more reactive the bronchial airway is. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Methacholine Challenge Test -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The methacholine challenge test is a bronchoprovocation test used to assess airway hyperresponsiveness and aid in the diagnosis of asthma. Methacholine, a derivative of acetylcholine, directly stimulates airway smooth muscle receptors, leading to bronchoconstriction. This test is instrumental in diagnosing asthma, especially in patients with atypical presentations or persistent symptoms despite normal baseline spirometry. [1] In some cases, airway hyperresponsiveness may be the only measurable indicator of asthma. Bronchoprovocation testing helps evaluate the severity of airway sensitivity and the patient's response to therapy. Among various bronchoprovocation methods, the methacholine challenge is the most commonly used. Other agents and techniques include bradykinin, mannitol, adenosine monophosphate, hypertonic saline, histamine, exercise, eucapnic voluntary hyperpnea, and antigen-specific challenges, such as aspirin or food additives. [2] Due to the limited commercial availability and adverse effects, clinicians primarily use histamine for research purposes. [3] [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Bronchodilators -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Anticholinergic drugs target receptors of the parasympathetic nervous system in the airways and inhibit their activity. As the parasympathetic nervous system is responsible for increased bronchial secretions and airway constriction, reversing these effects should provide bronchodilation and reduced secretions. The anticholinergic drugs predominantly target the M3 receptor. [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Antisialagogues -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: If conditions such as infection cause dysfunction of the M2 receptors, severe hyperreactivity of the M3 receptors may ensue, which then causes increased secretion and bronchoconstriction. M3 receptors are exquisitely sensitive to any cholinergic stimulation, while M2 receptors are much less sensitive. [11] Oral glycopyrronium (also known as glycopyrrolate) at doses of 4 and 8 mg respectively have demonstrated a profound reduction in the secretion of saliva between 3 and 6 hours of drug administration with 50% of baseline secretion at 6 hours. Peak effects generally appeared at around 30 minutes after drug administration. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Methacholine Challenge Test -- Personnel
retrieved_docs.content: Skilled technicians are crucial in guiding patients through the correct breathing maneuvers required for spirometry and accurate FEV 1 measurement. Poor technique can compromise test validity. Once methacholine administration begins, the patient should not be left unattended at any point. A clinician or other qualified healthcare provider capable of managing acute bronchospasm must be readily available to respond immediately in case of an emergency. Appropriately trained personnel must be present to ensure both patient safety and test accuracy.

retrieved_docs.title: Methacholine Challenge Test -- Preparation
retrieved_docs.content: Albuterol: 6 h Levalbuterol: 6 h Terbutaline: 6 h Formoterol and salmeterol: 36 h Ultra long-acting beta-agonists: 48 h Short-acting muscarinic antagonist such as ipratropium: 12 h Long-acting muscarinic antagonists such as aclidinium, glycopyrrolate, and tiotropium: 1 week Theophylline: 24 h Oral albuterol: 12 to 24 h Oral glucocorticoids: 2 to 3 weeks Cromolyn sodium: 4 h Leukotriene modifiers: 4 weeks Inhaled glucocorticoids: 4 weeks

retrieved_docs.title: Status Asthmaticus -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Cellular-level changes play a key role in the development of asthma symptoms. Destruction of cilia and epithelial denudation increase nerve ending irritability, resulting in hyperreactivity. Inflammation causes hypertrophy and hyperfunction of goblet cells and mucous glands, promoting mucus plugging within the airways. Dysregulated parasympathetic activity contributes to cellular pathology through pulmonary vagal branches and parasympathetic ganglia in small bronchi. Stimulation of postganglionic acetylcholine muscarinic receptors causes bronchoconstriction and hypersecretion. Dysfunction of inhibitory M2 receptors, commonly seen in individuals with atopy, sustained allergen exposure, viral infection, and chronic inflammation, further enhances parasympathetic activity.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Muscarinic Receptor -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Either inhibition or stimulation can treat several different diseases and disorders of muscarinic receptors in the affected area. Identifying muscarinic receptors involved in specific pathologies can allow for the development of antagonists and agonists to help decrease symptoms. In asthma and some COPD cases, a long-acting beta2 agonist (LABA) is used to upregulate sympathetic control in the airways and decrease constriction. Several add-on treatments for asthma have come onto the market throughout the years, and several of these include muscarinic receptor antagonists, also known as long-acting muscarinic antagonists (LAMAs). One of the most recently developed LAMA is tiotropium, which acts on M3 receptors. Tiotropium inhibits smooth muscle cells of the airways and submucosal glands to decrease airway constriction and mucus secretion. [21]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 4 -- Community 2734
retrieved_docs.content: Adrenergic beta‑Antagonists, whose mechanism of action involves blocking β‑adrenergic receptors, have a contraindicated mechanism of action with methacholin, a muscarinic receptor agonist. Because the pharmacologic effects of β‑blockade oppose the cholinergic stimulation produced by methacholin—potentially leading to adverse outcomes such as enhanced bronchoconstriction or undesirable cardiovascular effects—these agents should not be used together in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1450
retrieved_docs.content: Methacholin is identified as a specific bronchoconstrictive agent that belongs to the broader class of autonomic substances. Within the autonomic system, parasympathomimetics serve as a parent category, indicating that methacholin’s pharmacologic effects are mediated through parasympathetic stimulation. Because methacholin induces bronchoconstriction, the mechanism of action of adrenergic beta‑antagonists is contraindicated when methacholin is used, as beta‑blockers would oppose the desired therapeutic effect. The hierarchical relationships show that autonomic agents encompass both parasympathomimetic and bronchoconstrictive agents, situating methacholin at the intersection of these categories and highlighting the need to avoid beta‑adrenergic antagonists in patients receiving methacholin.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 374
retrieved_docs.content: Cholinergic Muscarinic Antagonists [MoA], meaning that it blocks muscarinic acetylcholine receptors in the airway smooth muscle, thereby preventing bronchoconstriction. This muscarinic antagonism is the primary pathway by which ipratropium bromide achieves bronchodilation. The combination of albuterol, a β2‑adrenergic agonist, with ipratropium bromide, a muscarinic antagonist, provides complementary bronchodilatory actions: albuterol stimulates relaxation of airway smooth muscle via β2 receptors, while ipratropium bromide inhibits cholinergic‑mediated constriction. Together, these agents enhance overall airway opening, which is clinically valuable in the management of obstructive lung diseases such as asthma and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD). A separate triple incorrectly linked the mechanism of action of mirtazapine to Cholinergic Muscarinic Antagonists [MoA]. In reality, mirtazapine’s pharmacologic profile involves antagonism of central α2‑adrenergic receptors and

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 637
retrieved_docs.content: Metaproterenol is classified as a non‑selective beta‑adrenoreceptor agonist inhalant, meaning it belongs to the group of inhaled medications that stimulate both β1 and β2 adrenergic receptors. As a member of this class, metaproterenol functions as a bronchodilator, providing relief of bronchospasm in conditions such as asthma and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease by relaxing airway smooth muscle. Recognizing metaproterenol’s membership in the non‑selective beta‑adrenoreceptor agonist inhalant category helps clinicians understand its pharmacologic profile, therapeutic uses, and potential side‑effect spectrum compared with more selective agents.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 0 -- Community 19
retrieved_docs.content: swelling and bronchoconstriction, highlighting its potential therapeutic role in histamine‑mediated conditions. This taxonomy clarifies systemic versus inhaled routes, adrenergic subtypes, and the composition of combination bronchodilator products, supporting clinicians in selecting appropriate pharmacotherapy for optimal management of obstructive airway diseases and related allergic manifestations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 2145
retrieved_docs.content: Adrenergic beta‑Antagonists [MoA] exert their pharmacologic effect by antagonizing beta‑1 adrenergic receptors, thereby reducing sympathetic stimulation of the heart and other tissues. Because this mechanism opposes the cholinergic activity of methacholin, the use of Adrenergic beta‑Antagonists [MoA] is contraindicated in patients receiving methacholin, as the conflicting actions can lead to adverse physiological effects. Clinicians should therefore avoid combining these agents to ensure safe and effective patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 1823
retrieved_docs.content: Autonomic substances encompass a category of agents known as bronchoconstrictive substances, which are responsible for causing narrowing of the airways through their action on the bronchial smooth muscle.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1359
retrieved_docs.content: Bronchokonstrikční látky constitute a class of pharmacologic agents that cause narrowing of the bronchial airways, and methacholin is identified as a specific member of this class, representing a narrower subset of bronchoconstrictive substances.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 3 -- Community 1955
retrieved_docs.content: Guaithylline is identified as a bronchodilator, meaning it promotes relaxation of airway smooth muscle and can be used to alleviate bronchoconstriction. In contrast, atropine is not classified as a bronchodilator; its pharmacological profile is inversely related to bronchodilation, reflecting an anticholinergic mechanism that influences airway tone differently. Recognizing that Guaithylline directly induces bronchodilation while atropine’s effects are opposite in nature assists clinicians in selecting appropriate agents for managing respiratory conditions involving airway constriction.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 2 -- Community 247
retrieved_docs.content: Selective beta‑2‑adrenoreceptor agonist inhalants constitute a pharmacologic class of inhaled bronchodilators that act by selectively stimulating β2‑adrenergic receptors in the airway smooth muscle. The agents bitolterol, carbuterol, fenoterol, klenbuterol and olodaterol are all members of this class. By targeting β2 receptors, these inhalants produce rapid relaxation of bronchial smooth muscle, providing symptomatic relief in obstructive airway diseases such as asthma and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD). Recognizing that each of these drugs belongs to the same selective β2‑agonist inhalant group helps clinicians understand their shared mechanism of action, therapeutic indications, and potential class‑related side effects, thereby supporting informed prescribing and optimal patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 639
retrieved_docs.content: Alpha- and beta‑adrenoreceptor agonist inhalants are a subclass of adrenergic inhalants. By stimulating both alpha and beta adrenoreceptors, these inhaled agents produce bronchodilation and are employed in the treatment of obstructive airway diseases such as asthma and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease. Their classification as adrenergic inhalants highlights their mechanism of activating sympathetic receptors to relax airway smooth muscle, thereby improving airflow and symptom control in respiratory therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 2039
retrieved_docs.content: Selective beta‑2‑adrenoreceptor agonist inhalants constitute a pharmacologic class of inhaled bronchodilators that act by selectively stimulating β2‑adrenergic receptors in the airway smooth muscle, leading to relaxation and improved airflow in obstructive lung diseases such as asthma and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease. The agents identified as members of this class include bitolterol, carbuterol, hexoprenalin, klenbuterol, and olodaterol. Each of these drugs shares the core mechanism of β2‑receptor activation but may differ in pharmacokinetic properties, duration of action, and clinical indications, providing clinicians with multiple options for tailoring bronchodilator therapy to individual patient needs. Recognizing that all these agents belong to the same selective β2‑adrenoreceptor inhalant group helps guide appropriate selection, dosing, and monitoring in respiratory care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1588
retrieved_docs.content: Selective beta‑2‑adrenoreceptor agonist inhalants constitute a pharmacologic class of bronchodilators that act specifically on β2‑adrenergic receptors in the airway smooth muscle to produce rapid relaxation and improve airflow. The agents bitolterol, carbuterol, isoetharin, klenbuterol, olodaterol, and reproterol are all members of this class. By selectively stimulating β2 receptors, these inhaled medications are used primarily in the management of obstructive airway diseases such as asthma and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD), providing symptom relief and facilitating better pulmonary function. Their shared mechanism of action and route of administration underscore their relevance in respiratory therapy and guide clinicians in selecting appropriate β2‑agonist inhalants for individualized patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 3 -- Community 1416
retrieved_docs.content: Selective beta‑2‑adrenoreceptor inhalants comprise a class of bronchodilator medications that act selectively on airway β2‑adrenergic receptors to relax smooth muscle and improve airflow in obstructive lung diseases such as asthma and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease. The agents bitolterol, carbuterol, fenoterol, olodaterol, prokaterol and tulobuterol are all members of this class. Recognizing that each of these drugs belongs to the selective β2‑adrenoreceptor inhalant category aids clinicians in selecting appropriate inhaled therapy and understanding their shared pharmacologic profile.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 1201
retrieved_docs.content: Selective beta‑2‑adrenoreceptor agonist inhalants constitute a pharmacologic class of bronchodilators that act by selectively stimulating β2‑adrenergic receptors in the airway smooth muscle, leading to relaxation and improved airflow in obstructive lung diseases such as asthma and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease. The agents bitolterol, carbuterol, indacaterol, isoetharin, olodaterol, reproterol, and tretochinol are all members of this class. By sharing the same mechanism of selective β2‑receptor activation, these inhalants provide rapid and sustained relief of bronchospasm, and their inclusion in the same therapeutic group informs clinicians that they have comparable pharmacodynamic properties, dosing considerations, and potential side‑effect profiles, thereby guiding appropriate selection and management of inhaled therapy for patients with respiratory obstruction.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 1582
retrieved_docs.content: Adrenergic beta‑Antagonists [MoA] function as antagonisté beta‑1‑adrenergních receptorů, blocking β1‑adrenergic stimulation. Their mechanism of action is contraindicated with methacholin, a specific bronchokonstrikční látky. Methacholin is classified among bronchokonstrikční látky, agents that cause bronchoconstriction through cholinergic pathways. Because β‑blockade can worsen bronchoconstriction or oppose the intended pharmacologic effect of methacholin, the use of Adrenergic beta‑Antagonists [MoA] is contraindicated when methacholin is administered. Recognizing this interaction is essential for safe patient care, particularly in clinical situations involving bronchoconstrictive therapies.
